(2) The Librarian/Consolidator as a role with ZERO code/merge power that only writes Semantic memory through the governed API -- graft this exactly; it is the cleanest answer to 'how do you have a fact store that can't poison your codebase.' (3) The honest two-mode Record-and-Replay (Audit/Reconstruct + Plan-Replay, never bit-for-bit) -- this is the strongest shared idea; graft lean-loop-first's exact framing of it. (4) The engine-as-platform north star (ledger+R&R+governed boundary sold to non-dev workloads) as the durable-moat narrative.

GRAFT (from lean-loop-first): The week-by-week 'close ONE loop on ONE repo with a human gate' P0/P1 BEFORE building the durable Semantic fact store. Ship the EPISODIC ledger from day one (cheap, append-only, IS the moat substrate) but DEFER Semantic-fact reconciliation + hybrid retrieval until the loop demonstrably re-runs and a real cross-session fact need appears. This resolves memory-moat-first's fatal inversion: you get the moat substrate (the ledger) immediately and the expensive consolidation engine only on proven demand. Also graft lean's flag-loop iteration cap + human escalation (mandatory cost control) and lean's atomic compare-and-set claim (race control).

GRAFT (from platform-first): The 'dry-run sandbox PR before you trust the team' onboarding moment -- this is the best GTM insight and de-risks adoption -- but DEFER it until after the single-tenant loop+ledger work, and do NOT build full multi-tenancy/RLS/billing/the visual builder until the loop has closed for the first design-partner tenant. Treat the composer/builder as the path to per-tenant customization, sequenced AFTER core-value proof.

CUT: (a) The lean design's instinct to defer the ledger -- reverse it; the ledger leads. (b) The memory design's P1 durable-fact engine -- push it behind loop validation. (c) The platform design's day-1 tenancy/RLS/sandbox/billing front-load -- single-tenant first, but write the tenant_id discriminator and the TenantA-cannot-read-TenantB test as the gating artifact the moment a second tenant is in sight (don't retrofit). (d) Across all: stop marketing 'cross-repo causal' and 'forum-as-coordination' as built pillars until a graph store and a real inter-agent thread mechanism are actually scoped -- either build them or rename them as roadmap, because as written they are vision-deck items the architecture defers.

NON-NEGOTIABLE for whichever plan ships: the merge gate must verify CI-green via a LIVE GitHub API call against the enforced required-check set at merge time (defense-in-depth with branch protection), never a passed-in boolean -- this is the entire safety story and all three leave it under-specified. And sequence at least minimal CI-egress/secret-scoping BEFORE onboarding tenant #2, since the code-executing agent's injection blast radius is live from the first real PR.

scores.notes: The only one of the three that is actually buildable on the real platform in weeks, because it explicitly stays single-tenant/single-repo/single-human and defers the parts the platform cannot support. It correctly intuits that GitHub is the page-store and the commit DAG is the temporal backbone, so it doesn't over-invest in a memory engine. BUT it shares the unexamined fatal assumption that the LoopRR API is the 'sole write boundary': on this platform the routine holds the GitHub connector and can call mcp__github__merge_pull_request / push_files DIRECTLY, bypassing mergeAuthorized entirely. Its own 'biggest_risks' names CI-green spoofing but MISSES that the merge verb itself is bypassable. Mitigation exists and is cheap here (rely on GitHub branch protection as the real gate, drop write-MCP, restrict to claude/ branch pushes), and because it keeps the human as the merge-clicker in v1, the bypass is contained — which is why feasibility stays high. Moat is deliberately thin (that's the point) so moat_strength is low by design, not by error.

Strategically the most differentiated and the cleanest reasoning on storage (Postgres+git as system of record, reject Zep/4-graph, dual-stream, soft-supersession with provenance). The moat thesis — a governed temporal+spatial flight-recorder for agent fleets — is genuinely defensible IF it could be built. The problem is the moat rests on a primitive the platform does not provide: there is NO completion callback (confirmed in docs AND migration 0010's own comment 'no completion callback beyond dispatch'), so server-stamped clock-OUT and server-derived duration are impossible. The ledger can only get clock-out from a self-reporting routine — i.e. the exact untrusted worker clock the design swears it will never trust. The 'immutable temporal backbone' the whole moat sits on is therefore built on unverifiable self-reports. Plan-Replay is honest, but 'who-was-where-for-how-long' integrity is not achievable as specified. Scope is heavy (5 roles incl. a Librarian, dual-stream, hybrid retrieval, R&R, multi-tenant) for a platform that can't even isolate tenants.

The 'compose your dream team' onboarding is a real wow and the RLS-first instinct is correct for the DB. But this angle collides head-on with the platform's hardest constraint and never acknowledges it: routines belong to ONE individual claude.ai account, are NOT shared, all actions appear AS the operator (commits/PRs carry the operator's GitHub user; connectors use the operator's linked accounts), and runs draw on ONE shared per-account daily cap. A multi-tenant SaaS where each tenant 'composes and runs their own fleet of Claude-Code routines' is therefore not realizable on routines as they exist — there is no per-tenant routine identity, no per-tenant connector identity, no per-tenant run metering, and tenant B's agents would act as the same human as tenant A's. DB-level tenant_id+RLS (which the design obsesses over) isolates the database but does nothing about the execution/identity layer, which is where the real multi-tenant leak and the real cost-attribution problem live. Highest product ambition, lowest platform-feasibility; ships last, not first.

fatal_flaws.flaw: The 'governed API as sole write boundary' is false on this platform for GIT writes. Every design keeps the GitHub MCP connector on the worker routine (mandatory, to read diffs/logs and open PRs), but that same connector exposes mcp__github__merge_pull_request, push_files, and create_or_update_file as directly-callable tools with NO per-action permission prompt (confirmed: routines docs state 'Claude can use every tool from an included connector, including writes, without asking'). A prompt-injected or simply over-eager routine can merge to main or push to any branch directly, bypassing mergeAuthorized/CI-green entirely. The DB gate governs the DB; it cannot govern git. The only real fixes are platform-native, not API-native: (a) DO NOT enable 'Allow unrestricted branch pushes' so routines can only push claude/* branches, and (b) enforce GitHub branch protection + required reviews as the actual merge gate, and/or (c) build a custom read-only GitHub MCP and proxy ALL git mutations through the LoopRR API with a server-held GitHub App token the routine never has. None of the three designs build (c) or even name the connector-bypass; design 2 and 3 explicitly claim the merge verb is the 'single path to main', which is untrue as specified.

No completion callback exists. The platform returns only a session id/url at dispatch; there is no webhook/callback when a run finishes (routines docs confirm; migration 0010 comment states it outright). Therefore: server-side clock-OUT, server-derived duration, 'live Open-session watching' as a product feature, and run-grouped 'who-was-where-for-how-long' feeds CANNOT be built from authoritative signals. Clock-out can only be (i) self-reported by the routine (the untrusted worker clock all three swear off), or (ii) inferred by timeout/heuristic. This is merely a UX gap for design 1, but it is load-bearing-and-broken for design 2, whose entire moat is an integrity-grade temporal+spatial ledger.
fatal_flaws.severity: fatal for memory-moat-first; major for the others

fatal_flaws.flaw: Multi-tenant-on-day-one is not realizable on routines. Routines are per individual claude.ai account, unshared, act AS the operator's GitHub+connector identity, and share one per-account daily run cap. tenant_id+RLS isolates the database but the execution layer has no tenant boundary: every tenant's 'team' runs as the same human, against the operator's connectors, metered on one shared cap. There is no supported way to give tenant B its own routine identity/credentials/quota. The design's headline ('run a fleet across your repos as multi-tenant SaaS') has no platform substrate.

The directive/work-assignment 'queue' delivered via the fire-URL text payload is an UNPARSED LITERAL STRING concatenated next to the routine's saved prompt (routines docs: 'not parsed... received as a literal string'). So the 'governed directive-as-data' assignment bus is, at the execution layer, a raw prompt-injection channel: whoever can POST to the fire-URL (or whatever untrusted issue/client text the routine ingests and echoes) injects instructions directly. For a role like the Implementer that then executes code and (per the connector-bypass flaw) can push/merge, this compounds into code-exec + supply-chain + secret-exfil. The design treats the queue as structured/trusted; it is neither.

The reactive Implementer<->Reviewer flag loop relies on GitHub PR events, but GitHub webhook events are subject to per-routine AND per-account hourly caps during the research preview, and events beyond the cap are SILENTLY DROPPED. Under any real concurrency the loop-back can stall mid-cycle with no error (a dropped synchronize/opened event), producing stuck tickets that look fine. The design's cap-loop-iterations mitigation addresses livelock but not silent event loss. Needs a reconciling heartbeat sweep (>=1h) that re-derives PR state from polling, not just event-driven wakeups.

cross_cutting_risks: Connector write-tool bypass: any routine with the GitHub connector can merge/push directly; the LoopRR DB-side merge gate is necessary but NOT sufficient. The enforceable gate is GitHub branch protection + 'claude/* branches only' (disable unrestricted pushes) + optionally a custom read-only GitHub MCP with all mutations proxied through a server-held App token. Budget this as a first-class build, not a footnote.

No completion callback: treat every run as fire-and-forget. Do not promise live duration/clock-out integrity. The ledger's authoritative events are clock-IN (server-stamped at the API call the routine makes on start) and any mid-run status POSTs; clock-OUT must be modeled as 'last-heartbeat + inferred-stale', explicitly marked non-authoritative, or derived from the terminal GitHub artifact (PR opened/updated) rather than a session-end signal.

Identity collapse on the platform: all routine actions are the operator's identity, on one account, under one daily run cap. This caps the product at single-operator / single-org scope until/unless Anthropic ships per-tenant routine identity. Multi-tenant SaaS billing, isolation, and attribution cannot be solved in the app DB alone.

Fire-URL text is unparsed literal text injected beside the prompt: it is an injection channel, not a typed RPC. Never let it carry trust; the structured work item must be FETCHED by the routine from the governed API (pull model) and the text payload used only as an opaque nudge. All three already lean pull-on-session-start, which is the correct mitigation — make it a hard rule.

GitHub event hourly caps with silent drops: event-driven coordination is best-effort, not reliable delivery. Every reactive loop needs a reconciling poll/heartbeat (>=1h, the platform minimum) that re-derives state from GitHub/DB so dropped events self-heal.

Execution blast radius is live and larger than Knovo's: a coding routine ingests untrusted issue/dep text AND executes shell AND (via connector) can write git. Network egress is allowlist-bounded by the environment (good, 403 host_not_allowed by default), but secret-exfil via CI and supply-chain via arbitrary deps remain. Per-tenant CI secret scoping + sandbox are prerequisites BEFORE any second principal, and on this platform 'second principal' is barely supported anyway.

Cost/runaway: one shared per-account daily run cap + subscription draw means an agent that loops (e.g. Reviewer<->Implementer) burns the shared allowance and can starve all other routines on the account. Meter agent-runs in-app and hard-cap loop iterations from v1, independent of any per-tenant billing fantasy.
judges.synthesis_recommendation: Anchor on lean-loop-first. It is the only design whose scope matches what the platform can actually do today, and its phase gates are honest. Build the v1 exactly as it proposes (3 routines, one repo, one human, human-clicked merge), with FOUR mandatory hardening grafts that fix flaws lean-loop-first shares but underweights: (1) The real merge gate is GitHub branch protection + required reviews + 'disable unrestricted branch pushes' (routines push only claude/* branches), NOT the DB-side mergeAuthorized alone — because the GitHub connector exposes merge_pull_request directly. Treat the DB gate as bookkeeping/audit, and the GitHub-native protections as the enforcement. As a phase-2 hardening, build a read-only GitHub MCP + a server-side git-write proxy through the LoopRR API holding the App token, so the routine never carries write tools at all. (2) Make 'pull-on-session-start' a hard invariant: routines FETCH structured work from the governed API; the fire-URL text payload is treated as untrusted opaque text only, never parsed as a command. (3) Redefine the ledger honestly per the no-callback reality: clock-IN and status are authoritative (server-stamped when the routine calls the API); clock-OUT is inferred/non-authoritative and the team-calendar surfaces 'last seen' not 'duration worked'. This pre-empts design 2's fatal flaw. (4) Add a >=1h reconciling heartbeat sweep that re-derives PR/ticket state from polling, so silently-dropped GitHub events self-heal; cap loop-back iterations and meter runs from v1 to bound the shared daily cap. From memory-moat-first, GRAFT only the storage discipline and the two-named-replay-modes framing (Audit/Reconstruct vs Plan-Replay) as the Phase-3 honest target — these are its best, real contributions — but DROP its claim to an integrity-grade duration ledger and its 5-role/dual-stream/Librarian apparatus until a workload exists. From platform-first, GRAFT nothing for v1 except the RLS-isolation-test discipline as a LATCH for if/when multi-tenancy becomes possible; CUT multi-tenant-on-day-one entirely — it has no platform substrate (single-account, single-identity, single daily cap), so it must be reframed as single-operator/single-org until Anthropic ships per-tenant routine identity, and that reframing should be written into the roadmap as an explicit platform dependency, not an app-layer milestone. Net: ship lean-loop-first's loop, enforce the gate in GitHub not just the DB, tell the truth about the ledger, and shelve the moat and the SaaS until the platform (or a self-hosted execution layer) can carry them."

scores.notes: The only design whose sequencing respects Knovo's actual state (Phase 1 code-complete, harness unbuilt, single-tenant). Phase 0 = repoint the spine in 1-2 wks is the most honest claim in the packet: worker-auth.ts is 73 lines, worker-api.ts is 133, both genuinely re-targetable. Correctly defers the four-type memory engine, multi-tenancy, R&R-replay, and graph store. The critical path to a closing loop is real. BUT it under-prices two genuinely-new builds smuggled into 'Phase 1': (a) the second git write boundary + Supervisor reconciliation, which Knovo lists as M1+M2 UNBUILT design, and (b) the CI-green merge precondition, which is NOT an extension of publishAuthorized (a pure DB-state function) but a new live-external-signal dependency. Phase 1 'weeks 3-5' is optimistic by the cost of those two. Moat is deliberately thin in v1 and the design never states WHEN the moat arrives or what defends the loop from commoditization meanwhile.

Strongest articulation of the durable asset and the honest two-mode R&R split. But as a SEQUENCING plan it is inverted and over-built. P0 bundles the governed boundary + immutable ledger + the SECOND git boundary (branch protection + merge verb + CI-green) + full RLS tenant isolation into one phase — three unbuilt subsystems Knovo's own roadmap separates across M1, M2, and M5. P1 is a full memory engine (dual-stream, Semantic facts, Mem0 reconciliation, hybrid retrieval, degradation fallback, GAM researcher, Librarian role) BEFORE the dev loop has demonstrably closed end-to-end — the exact 'over-built memory engine' failure the lens hunts for. The design concedes 'a memory engine with no workload is a toy' then sequences the engine before the workload. The Librarian is a 5th role shipped before a single merged PR exists to consolidate facts from.

Worst scope-and-sequencing offender. P0 is 'tenancy + governance skeleton: tenant_id on EVERY table, is_tenant_admin RLS, per-(tenant,role) tokens' — Knovo's M5, which its roadmap explicitly gates behind M1-M4 AND single-tenant validation ('pull NONE of M1-M6 until the Knovo tenant is validated'). Building multi-tenancy first, before any loop has closed, is textbook premature multi-tenancy: you pay the RLS-everywhere tax, the cross-tenant isolation burden, and per-tenant token resolution on day zero with zero validated product underneath. The 'compose your dream team' builder + onboarding interview + sandbox dry-run (P3) rests on the deferred parametric-prompt composer (M3, unbuilt) — a wow-moment with no proven loop behind it. Billing-readiness as a day-one concern compounds the inversion. The onboarding-wedge thesis may be commercially right long-term, but the sequencing ships the storefront before the product works once.

fatal_flaws.flaw: Premature multi-tenancy as Phase 0. P0 puts tenant_id on every table + is_tenant_admin RLS + per-(tenant,role) tokens + the cross-tenant isolation test BEFORE a single autonomous loop has ever closed. Knovo's roadmap.md names this work M5 and explicitly gates it: 'pull none of M1-M6 until the Knovo tenant is validated... Phase 1 working in prod is the runway, not the climb.' You inherit a single-tenant schema with NO discriminator anywhere (data-model.md: 'The schema today is single-tenant — there is no tenant discriminator anywhere. Nothing below is built'), pay the full RLS-everywhere + isolation-test tax on day zero, and slow every subsequent phase, all to support a product not shown to work even once. This is the canonical inversion the scope-and-sequencing lens exists to catch.

The headline wow-moment (P3 'compose your dream team' builder + Conductor onboarding interview + sandbox dry-run) is built on the parametric-prompt composer, which is Knovo M3 — unbuilt 'future' design (worker-harness.md §7) that itself depends on the M1/M2 harness substrate. The design ships an elaborate self-serve storefront and visual team-builder before proving the loop closes once, stacked on three layers of unbuilt substrate (harness, Supervisor, composer). Time-to-first-green-PR cannot be the north-star metric when the green-PR pipeline is itself unbuilt beneath the onboarding flow.

Over-built memory engine sequenced ahead of the workload that justifies it. P1 ships the full dual-stream write path, Semantic facts with Mem0 ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP reconciliation, hybrid retrieval with three-level degradation fallback, the GAM researcher, and a dedicated 5th Librarian/Consolidator role — all BEFORE the dev loop has demonstrably produced a merged PR (loop-close is asserted, not gated, before P1). The design self-refutes: it states 'a memory engine with no workload is a toy' then sequences the engine before the workload is proven. There are no facts to consolidate until the loop has run; building the consolidator first is infrastructure for a user that doesn't exist yet.

P0 over-bundling. It fuses the governed DB boundary + immutable ledger + the entire SECOND git write boundary (branch protection + merge verb + CI-green precondition) + the full RLS TenantA/TenantB isolation gate into one phase. Knovo separates these across M1 (harness), M2 (read-write coordination + Supervisor), and M5 (tenancy). Treating CI-green as 'extending publishAuthorized' understates it: publishAuthorized (lib/worker-api.ts) is a pure function of DB status + open directives with zero external-signal plumbing — adding a live, non-spoofable, branch-protection-consistent CI check is a new subsystem, not an extension. P0 as scoped is several phases of work mislabeled as one.

Under-prices two genuinely-new builds folded into 'Phase 1 (weeks 3-5)': (1) the second git write boundary plus Supervisor-style reconciliation — which in Knovo is M1+M2, entirely 'design', and which makes 'feature branch as no-race boundary' safe; the design defers the Supervisor to Phase 2 while relying on its safety property in Phase 1, a sequencing contradiction (who reconciles concurrent branch work in Phase 1?). (2) The CI-green merge precondition as a new live-signal dependency, not an extension. The loop can close in weeks, but not at the confidence the 'weeks 3-5' label implies once these are costed honestly.

Moat-timing is unstated. By correctly cutting memory/R&R/tenancy to later phases, v1 is a thin governance wrapper over GitHub that a competitor could clone quickly. The design defers the moat without committing to a phase where differentiation must materialize or a signal that triggers it — risking a validated-but-undefensible loop that never graduates to the durable asset.

cross_cutting_risks: All three designs treat 'extend publishAuthorized with CI-green' as a trivial graft. Ground truth (lib/worker-api.ts): publishAuthorized is a pure function of artifact status + open directives — no external-signal input, no notion of git, no liveness/spoofing model. A correct CI-green gate must read live check state at merge time, require the specific required-check set, and stay consistent with GitHub branch protection (defense in depth). This is a shared, under-scoped new subsystem in every plan and belongs on the true critical path of whichever loop ships first.

The 'repo is read-only context' invariant is the backbone of Knovo's architecture (worker-harness.md §1: 'The data path is the API; the repo is context'). All three designs invert it — workers now write code — and all three wave at 'feature branch replaces subfolder as the no-race boundary.' But the reconciliation actor (Supervisor) that makes scoped writes safe is unbuilt 'design' (M2). None of the three has a credible Phase-1 answer for concurrent writes to shared files before the Supervisor exists; they assume the property they haven't built.

Knovo is Phase 1 code-complete but operationally UNRUN (roadmap.md: 'Phase 1 (code-complete, operationally unrun)'). Every design's Phase 0 timeline assumes the inherited spine works in production; it has never run the governed loop live even in its native domain. The first real risk for all three is that the borrowed spine itself needs hardening before any dev-loop work begins — none of the three budgets for this.

Prompt-injection blast radius inverts from 'writes validated JSON' to 'executes code + opens PRs that run in CI.' All three correctly name sandboxing/egress/scoped-CI-secrets but all three defer them to the LAST phase (P4/Phase 4). For lean-loop-first this is defensible (single trusted owner, own repo); for memory-moat and platform-first, which onboard multiple distrusting tenants earlier, deferring execution isolation to the final phase means the multi-tenant capability ships before the isolation that makes it safe — a sequencing hazard, not just a backlog item.

Every design promotes the Supervisor/Tech-Lead/Conductor from Knovo's 'design-only' status to a first-class shipped role, but none reckons with the fact that this role is the single biggest unbuilt subsystem in Knovo (M2 in full). It is the linchpin of the git coordination boundary; treating it as a re-point rather than a new build is shared optimism baked into all three timelines.
judges.synthesis_recommendation:
Anchor on lean-loop-first — it is the only design whose sequencing matches the codebase's actual state (small re-pointable spine; harness, Supervisor, tenancy all unbuilt) and the only one with a real critical path to a closing loop. Treat its Phase 0 (repoint the 73-line auth + 133-line api spine, strip the molecular kit, artifact->ticket) as correct and genuinely a 1-2 week job.

Fix lean-loop-first's two understated builds before trusting its timeline: (1) Pull a MINIMAL Supervisor/Tech-Lead into Phase 1, not Phase 2 — you cannot honestly claim 'feature branch as no-race boundary' in Phase 1 while deferring the only actor that reconciles concurrent work. Scope it to the minimum: single-writer merge actor + git pull --rebase, nothing more. (2) Treat the CI-green merge precondition as a first-class Phase-1 deliverable with its own gate (live check read at merge time, required-check set enforced, branch protection as defense-in-depth), not an 'extension of publishAuthorized.' Re-label Phase 1 as ~weeks 3-6 to absorb these.

Graft from memory-moat-first exactly TWO things, as cheap scaffolding not the engine: (a) the thin episodic clock-in ledger (single Postgres table, server-stamped UTC, (repo,branch,worktree) spatial key) — lean-loop already includes this; keep it as the cheapest down-payment on the moat; and (b) the honest two-mode naming discipline (Audit/Reconstruct vs Plan-Replay) adopted as a NAMING + input-capture convention from day one (capture inputs+SHAs per run) even though the replay feature is deferred — near-zero cost, prevents the determinism over-promise. Defer everything else memory-moat proposes (dual-stream consolidation, Semantic facts, Mem0 reconciliation, hybrid retrieval, the Librarian role, graph store) to a post-loop phase gated explicitly on 'the loop has produced N merged PRs across M sessions' — make the workload prove itself before building the engine on top.

Cut platform-first's sequencing wholesale: do NOT build multi-tenancy, RLS-everywhere, per-tenant tokens, the team-builder, onboarding interview, or billing until the single-tenant loop is validated in production — this is Knovo's own held gate and inverting it is the plan's fatal flaw. PRESERVE platform-first's onboarding-wedge THESIS as the eventual go-to-market north star and its insight that time-to-first-green-PR is the right success metric, but schedule the composer/builder/onboarding as the phase AFTER the loop+moat exist, on the by-then-real parametric composer.

Net phase order: P0 repoint spine (single-tenant, single-repo) -> P1 close the loop with a minimal Supervisor + real CI-green gate + episodic ledger + run-input capture -> P2 promote memory engine ONLY on a merged-PR-count gate -> P3 onboarding/team-builder/composer -> P4 multi-tenant + execution sandboxing together (never tenancy without isolation). Keep the moat (ledger from P1, engine at P2) so lean-loop's thin-wrapper commoditization risk is answered, but never let the moat or the storefront precede a loop that has closed at least once."
